content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /home/briney/.claude/plugins/cache/claude-plugins-official/superpowers/6.1.0/skills/subagent-driven-development

# Subagent-Driven Development

Execute plan by dispatching a fresh implementer subagent per task, a task review (spec compliance + code quality) after each, and a broad whole-branch review at the end.

**Why subagents:** You delegate tasks to specialized agents with isolated context. By precisely crafting their instructions and context, you ensure they stay focused and succeed at their task. They should never inherit your session's context or history — you construct exactly what they need. This also preserves your own context for coordination work.

**Core principle:** Fresh subagent per task + task review (spec + quality) + broad final review = high quality, fast iteration

**Narration:** between tool calls, narrate at most one short line — the
ledger and the tool results carry the record.

**Continuous execution:** Do not pause to check in with your human partner between tasks. Execute all tasks from the plan without stopping. The only reasons to stop are: BLOCKED status you cannot resolve, ambiguity that genuinely prevents progress, or all tasks complete. "Should I continue?" prompts and progress summaries waste their time — they asked you to execute the plan, so execute it.

## When to Use

```dot
digraph when_to_use {
    "Have implementation plan?" [shape=diamond];
    "Tasks mostly independent?" [shape=diamond];
    "Stay in this session?" [shape=diamond];
    "subagent-driven-development" [shape=box];
    "executing-plans" [shape=box];
    "Manual execution or brainstorm first" [shape=box];

    "Have implementation plan?" -> "Tasks mostly independent?" [label="yes"];
    "Have implementation plan?" -> "Manual execution or brainstorm first" [label="no"];
    "Tasks mostly independent?" -> "Stay in this session?" [label="yes"];
    "Tasks mostly independent?" -> "Manual execution or brainstorm first" [label="no - tightly coupled"];
    "Stay in this session?" -> "subagent-driven-development" [label="yes"];
    "Stay in this session?" -> "executing-plans" [label="no - parallel session"];
}
```

**vs. Executing Plans (parallel session):**
- Same session (no context switch)
- Fresh subagent per task (no context pollution)
- Review after each task (spec compliance + code quality), broad review at the end
- Faster iteration (no human-in-loop between tasks)

## The Process

```dot
digraph process {
    rankdir=TB;

    subgraph cluster_per_task {
        label="Per Task";
        "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)" [shape=box];
        "Implementer subagent asks questions?" [shape=diamond];
        "Answer questions, provide context" [shape=box];
        "Implementer subagent implements, tests, commits, self-reviews" [shape=box];
        "Write diff file, dispatch task reviewer subagent (./task-reviewer-prompt.md)" [shape=box];
        "Task reviewer reports spec ✅ and quality approved?" [shape=diamond];
        "Dispatch fix subagent for Critical/Important findings" [shape=box];
        "Mark task complete in todo list and progress ledger" [shape=box];
    }

    "Read plan, note context and global constraints, create todos" [shape=box];
    "More tasks remain?" [shape=diamond];
    "Dispatch final code reviewer subagent (../requesting-code-review/code-reviewer.md)" [shape=box];
    "Use superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch" [shape=box style=filled fillcolor=lightgreen];

    "Read plan, note context and global constraints, create todos" -> "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)";
    "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)" -> "Implementer subagent asks questions?";
    "Implementer subagent asks questions?" -> "Answer questions, provide context" [label="yes"];
    "Answer questions, provide context" -> "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)";
    "Implementer subagent asks questions?" -> "Implementer subagent implements, tests, commits, self-reviews" [label="no"];
    "Implementer subagent implements, tests, commits, self-reviews" -> "Write diff file, dispatch task reviewer subagent (./task-reviewer-prompt.md)";
    "Write diff file, dispatch task reviewer subagent (./task-reviewer-prompt.md)" -> "Task reviewer reports spec ✅ and quality approved?";
    "Task reviewer reports spec ✅ and quality approved?" -> "Dispatch fix subagent for Critical/Important findings" [label="no"];
    "Dispatch fix subagent for Critical/Important findings" -> "Write diff file, dispatch task reviewer subagent (./task-reviewer-prompt.md)" [label="re-review"];
    "Task reviewer reports spec ✅ and quality approved?" -> "Mark task complete in todo list and progress ledger" [label="yes"];
    "Mark task complete in todo list and progress ledger" -> "More tasks remain?";
    "More tasks remain?" -> "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)" [label="yes"];
    "More tasks remain?" -> "Dispatch final code reviewer subagent (../requesting-code-review/code-reviewer.md)" [label="no"];
    "Dispatch final code reviewer subagent (../requesting-code-review/code-reviewer.md)" -> "Use superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch";
}
```

## Pre-Flight Plan Review

Before dispatching Task 1, scan the plan once for conflicts:

- tasks that contradict each other or the plan's Global Constraints
- anything the plan explicitly mandates that the review rubric treats as a
  defect (a test that asserts nothing, verbatim duplication of a logic block)

Present everything you find to your human partner as one batched question —
each finding beside the plan text that mandates it, asking which governs —
before execution begins, not one interrupt per discovery mid-plan. If the
scan is clean, proceed without comment. The review loop remains the net for
conflicts that only emerge from implementation.

## Model Selection

Use the least powerful model that can handle each role to conserve cost and increase speed.

**Mechanical implementation tasks** (isolated functions, clear specs, 1-2 files): use a fast, cheap model. Most implementation tasks are mechanical when the plan is well-specified.

**Integration and judgment tasks** (multi-file coordination, pattern matching, debugging): use a standard model.

**Architecture and design tasks**: use the most capable available model.
The final whole-branch review is one of these — dispatch it on the most
capable available model, not the session default.

**Review tasks**: choose the model with the same judgment, scaled to the
diff's size, complexity, and risk. A small mechanical diff does not need the
most capable model; a subtle concurrency change does.

**Always specify the model explicitly when dispatching a subagent.** An
omitted model inherits your session's model — often the most capable and
most expensive — which silently defeats this section.

**Turn count beats token price.** Wall-clock and context cost scale with how
many turns a subagent takes, and the cheapest models routinely take 2-3× the
turns on multi-step work — costing more overall. Use a mid-tier model as the
floor for reviewers and for implementers working from prose descriptions.
When the task's plan text contains the complete code to write, the
implementation is transcription plus testing: use the cheapest tier for
that implementer. Single-file mechanical fixes also take the cheapest tier.

**Task complexity signals (implementation tasks):**
- Touches 1-2 files with a complete spec → cheap model
- Touches multiple files with integration concerns → standard model
- Requires design judgment or broad codebase understanding → most capable model

## Handling Implementer Status

Implementer subagents report one of four statuses. Handle each appropriately:

**DONE:** Generate the review package (`scripts/review-package BASE HEAD`, from this skill's directory — it prints the unique file path it wrote; BASE is the commit you recorded before dispatching the implementer — never `HEAD~1`, which silently drops all but the last commit of a multi-commit task), then dispatch the task reviewer with the printed path.

**DONE_WITH_CONCERNS:** The implementer completed the work but flagged doubts. Read the concerns before proceeding. If the concerns are about correctness or scope, address them before review. If they're observations (e.g., "this file is getting large"), note them and proceed to review.

**NEEDS_CONTEXT:** The implementer needs information that wasn't provided. Provide the missing context and re-dispatch.

**BLOCKED:** The implementer cannot complete the task. Assess the blocker:
1. If it's a context problem, provide more context and re-dispatch with the same model
2. If the task requires more reasoning, re-dispatch with a more capable model
3. If the task is too large, break it into smaller pieces
4. If the plan itself is wrong, escalate to the human

**Never** ignore an escalation or force the same model to retry without changes. If the implementer said it's stuck, something needs to change.

## Handling Reviewer ⚠️ Items

The task reviewer may report "⚠️ Cannot verify from diff" items — requirements
that live in unchanged code or span tasks. These do not block the rest of the
review, but you must resolve each one yourself before marking the task
complete: you hold the plan and cross-task context the reviewer
lacks. If you confirm an item is a real gap, treat it as a failed spec
review — send it back to the implementer and re-review.

## Constructing Reviewer Prompts

Per-task reviews are task-scoped gates. The broad review happens once, at the
final whole-branch review. When you fill a reviewer template:

- Do not add open-ended directives like "check all uses" or "run race tests
  if useful" without a concrete, task-specific reason
- Do not ask a reviewer to re-run tests the implementer already ran on the
  same code — the implementer's report carries the test evidence
- Do not pre-judge findings for the reviewer — never instruct a reviewer to
  ignore or not flag a specific issue. If you believe a finding would be a
  false positive, let the reviewer raise it and adjudicate it in the review
  loop. If the prompt you are writing contains "do not flag," "don't treat X
  as a defect," "at most Minor," or "the plan chose" — stop: you are
  pre-judging, usually to spare yourself a review loop.
- The global-constraints block you hand the reviewer is its attention
  lens. Copy the binding requirements verbatim from the plan's Global
  Constraints section or the spec: exact values, exact formats, and the
  stated relationships between components ("same layout as X", "matches
  Y"). The reviewer's template already carries the process rules (YAGNI,
  test hygiene, review method) — the constraints block is for what THIS
  project's spec demands.
- Hand the reviewer its diff as a file: run this skill's
  `scripts/review-package BASE HEAD` and pass the reviewer the file path
  it prints (or, without bash: `git log --oneline`, `git diff --stat`,
  and `git diff -U10` for the range, redirected to one uniquely named
  file). The output never enters your own context, and the reviewer sees
  the commit list, stat summary, and full diff with context in one Read
  call. Use the BASE you recorded before dispatching the implementer —
  never `HEAD~1`, which silently truncates multi-commit tasks.
- A dispatch prompt describes one task, not the session's history. Do not
  paste accumulated prior-task summaries ("state after Tasks 1-3") into
  later dispatches — a real session's dispatch hit 42k chars of which 99%
  was pasted history. A fresh subagent needs its task, the interfaces it
  touches, and the global constraints. Nothing else.
- Dispatch fix subagents for Critical and Important findings. Record Minor
  findings in the progress ledger as you go, and point the final
  whole-branch review at that list so it can triage which must be fixed
  before merge. A roll-up nobody reads is a silent discard.
- A finding labeled plan-mandated — or any finding that conflicts with
  what the plan's text requires — is the human's decision, like any plan
  contradiction: present the finding and the plan text, ask which governs.
  Do not dismiss the finding because the plan mandates it, and do not
  dispatch a fix that contradicts the plan without asking.
- The final whole-branch review gets a package too: run
  `scripts/review-package MERGE_BASE HEAD` (MERGE_BASE = the commit the
  branch started from, e.g. `git merge-base main HEAD`) and include the
  printed path in the final review dispatch, so the final reviewer reads
  one file instead of re-deriving the branch diff with git commands.
- Every fix dispatch carries the implementer contract: the fix subagent
  re-runs the tests covering its change and reports the results. Name the
  covering test files in the dispatch — a one-line fix does not need the
  whole suite. Before re-dispatching the reviewer, confirm the fix report
  contains the covering tests, the command run, and the output; dispatch
  the re-review once all three are present.
- If the final whole-branch review returns findings, dispatch ONE fix
  subagent with the complete findings list — not one fixer per finding.
  Per-finding fixers each rebuild context and re-run suites; a real
  session's final-review fix wave cost more than all its tasks combined.

## File Handoffs

Everything you paste into a dispatch prompt — and everything a subagent
prints back — stays resident in your context for the rest of the session
and is re-read on every later turn. Hand artifacts over as files:

- **Task brief:** before dispatching an implementer, run this skill's
  `scripts/task-brief PLAN_FILE N` — it extracts the task's full text to a
  uniquely named file and prints the path. Compose the dispatch so the
  brief stays the single source of requirements. Your dispatch should
  contain: (1) one line on where this task fits in the project; (2) the
  brief path, introduced as "read this first — it is your requirements,
  with the exact values to use verbatim"; (3) interfaces and decisions
  from earlier tasks that the brief cannot know; (4) your resolution of
  any ambiguity you noticed in the brief; (5) the report-file path and
  report contract. Exact values (numbers, magic strings, signatures, test
  cases) appear only in the brief.
- **Report file:** name the implementer's report file after the brief
  (brief `…/task-N-brief.md` → report `…/task-N-report.md`) and put it in
  the dispatch prompt. The implementer writes the full report there and
  returns only status, commits, a one-line test summary, and concerns.
- **Reviewer inputs:** the task reviewer gets three paths — the same brief
  file, the report file, and the review package — plus the global
  constraints that bind the task.
- Fix dispatches append their fix report (with test results) to the same
  report file and return a short summary; re-reviews read the updated file.

## Durable Progress

Conversation memory does not survive compaction. In real sessions,
controllers that lost their place have re-dispatched entire completed task
sequences — the single most expensive failure observed. Track progress in
a ledger file, not only in todos.

- At skill start, check for a ledger:
  `cat "$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)/.superpowers/sdd/progress.md"`. Tasks listed there
  as complete are DONE — do not re-dispatch them; resume at the first task
  not marked complete.
- When a task's review comes back clean, append one line to the ledger in
  the same message as your other bookkeeping:
  `Task N: complete (commits <base7>..<head7>, review clean)`.
- The ledger is your recovery map: the commits it names exist in git even
  when your context no longer remembers creating them. After compaction,
  trust the ledger and `git log` over your own recollection.
- `git clean -fdx` will destroy the ledger (it's git-ignored scratch); if
  that happens, recover from `git log`.

## Prompt Templates

- [implementer-prompt.md](implementer-prompt.md) - Dispatch implementer subagent
- [task-reviewer-prompt.md](task-reviewer-prompt.md) - Dispatch task reviewer subagent (spec compliance + code quality)
- Final whole-branch review: use superpowers:requesting-code-review's [code-reviewer.md](../requesting-code-review/code-reviewer.md)

## Example Workflow

```
You: I'm using Subagent-Driven Development to execute this plan.

[Read plan file once: docs/superpowers/plans/feature-plan.md]
[Create todos for all tasks]

Task 1: Hook installation script

[Run task-brief for Task 1; dispatch implementer with brief + report paths + context]

Implementer: "Before I begin - should the hook be installed at user or system level?"

You: "User level (~/.config/superpowers/hooks/)"

Implementer: "Got it. Implementing now..."
[Later] Implementer:
  - Implemented install-hook command
  - Added tests, 5/5 passing
  - Self-review: Found I missed --force flag, added it
  - Committed

[Run review-package, dispatch task reviewer with the printed path]
Task reviewer: Spec ✅ - all requirements met, nothing extra.
  Strengths: Good test coverage, clean. Issues: None. Task quality: Approved.

[Mark Task 1 complete]

Task 2: Recovery modes

[Run task-brief for Task 2; dispatch implementer with brief + report paths + context]

Implementer: [No questions, proceeds]
Implementer:
  - Added verify/repair modes
  - 8/8 tests passing
  - Self-review: All good
  - Committed

[Run review-package, dispatch task reviewer with the printed path]
Task reviewer: Spec ❌:
  - Missing: Progress reporting (spec says "report every 100 items")
  - Extra: Added --json flag (not requested)
  Issues (Important): Magic number (100)

[Dispatch fix subagent with all findings]
Fixer: Removed --json flag, added progress reporting, extracted PROGRESS_INTERVAL constant

[Task reviewer reviews again]
Task reviewer: Spec ✅. Task quality: Approved.

[Mark Task 2 complete]

...

[After all tasks]
[Dispatch final code-reviewer]
Final reviewer: All requirements met, ready to merge

Done!
```

## Advantages

**vs. Manual execution:**
- Subagents follow TDD naturally
- Fresh context per task (no confusion)
- Parallel-safe (subagents don't interfere)
- Subagent can ask questions (before AND during work)

**vs. Executing Plans:**
- Same session (no handoff)
- Continuous progress (no waiting)
- Review checkpoints automatic

**Efficiency gains:**
- Controller curates exactly what context is needed; bulk artifacts move
  as files, not pasted text
- Subagent gets complete information upfront
- Questions surfaced before work begins (not after)

**Quality gates:**
- Self-review catches issues before handoff
- Task review carries two verdicts: spec compliance and code quality
- Review loops ensure fixes actually work
- Spec compliance prevents over/under-building
- Code quality ensures implementation is well-built

**Cost:**
- More subagent invocations (implementer + reviewer per task)
- Controller does more prep work (extracting all tasks upfront)
- Review loops add iterations
- But catches issues early (cheaper than debugging later)

## Red Flags

**Never:**
- Start implementation on main/master branch without explicit user consent
- Skip task review, or accept a report missing either verdict (spec compliance AND task quality are both required)
- Proceed with unfixed issues
- Dispatch multiple implementation subagents in parallel (conflicts)
- Make a subagent read the whole plan file (hand it its task brief —
  `scripts/task-brief` — instead)
- Skip scene-setting context (subagent needs to understand where task fits)
- Ignore subagent questions (answer before letting them proceed)
- Accept "close enough" on spec compliance (reviewer found spec issues = not done)
- Skip review loops (reviewer found issues = implementer fixes = review again)
- Let implementer self-review replace actual review (both are needed)
- Tell a reviewer what not to flag, or pre-rate a finding's severity in the
  dispatch prompt ("treat it as Minor at most") — the plan's example code is
  a starting point, not evidence that its weaknesses were chosen
- Dispatch a task reviewer without a diff file — generate it first
  (`scripts/review-package BASE HEAD`) and name the printed path in the
  prompt
- Move to next task while the review has open Critical/Important issues
- Re-dispatch a task the progress ledger already marks complete — check
  the ledger (and `git log`) after any compaction or resume

**If subagent asks questions:**
- Answer clearly and completely
- Provide additional context if needed
- Don't rush them into implementation

**If reviewer finds issues:**
- Implementer (same subagent) fixes them
- Reviewer reviews again
- Repeat until approved
- Don't skip the re-review

**If subagent fails task:**
- Dispatch fix subagent with specific instructions
- Don't try to fix manually (context pollution)

## Integration

**Required workflow skills:**
- **superpowers:using-git-worktrees** - Ensures isolated workspace (creates one or verifies existing)
- **superpowers:writing-plans** - Creates the plan this skill executes
- **superpowers:requesting-code-review** - Code review template for the final whole-branch review
- **superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch** - Complete development after all tasks

**Subagents should use:**
- **superpowers:test-driven-development** - Subagents follow TDD for each task

**Alternative workflow:**
- **superpowers:executing-plans** - Use for parallel session instead of same-session execution

input.prompt:
You are applying a correctness fix to Task 6's `long_range_precision_at_l` in `src/protlms/eval/contacts.py`.

## Environment
Use `python -m pytest ...` (bare `pytest` is the wrong linuxbrew interpreter). `python`/`pip` on PATH are the correct conda env.

## The bug
`long_range_precision_at_l(pred, true, resnums, *, sep, top=None)` currently computes the number of top pairs as:
```python
    k = i.size if top is None else min(int(top), i.size)
```
Here `i` (from `np.triu_indices` after the separation filter) is the list of ELIGIBLE PAIRS, so `i.size` is the eligible-pair count. But the metric is **precision@L where L = N = number of residues** (the function's own docstring says "top: Number of top pairs to score; N (= L) if None"). Defaulting to the eligible-pair count means a real protein (e.g. 100 residues → hundreds of eligible long-range pairs) would score ALL pairs, not the top-L — that is not precision@L. It only looks right in the existing test because that case coincidentally has 6 residues and 6 eligible pairs.

## The fix (do this via TDD)

1. FIRST add these two tests to `tests/test_eval_contacts.py` (they use the module's `long_range_precision_at_l`; `import pytest` and `import numpy as np` are already present):

```python
def test_long_range_precision_at_l_default_top_is_residue_count_not_pair_count() -> None:
    from protlms.eval.contacts import long_range_precision_at_l

    n = 6
    resnums = np.arange(n)  # sep=1 => all 15 upper-triangle pairs eligible
    pred = np.zeros((n, n), dtype=float)
    # top-6 pairs by score (descending): 3 true, 3 false among them
    pred[0, 1] = 0.9  # true
    pred[0, 2] = 0.8  # false
    pred[0, 3] = 0.7  # true
    pred[0, 4] = 0.6  # false
    pred[0, 5] = 0.5  # true
    pred[1, 2] = 0.4  # false
    true = np.zeros((n, n), dtype=bool)
    for a, b in [(0, 1), (0, 3), (0, 5), (1, 3), (2, 4), (3, 5)]:
        true[a, b] = True  # 6 true total; the last 3 have pred 0 => ranked below the top-6
    # default top must be L = N = 6, NOT the 15 eligible pairs.
    # precision@6 = 3 true in the top-6 / 6 = 0.5 (would be 6/15 if it scored all eligible pairs).
    assert long_range_precision_at_l(pred, true, resnums, sep=1) == 0.5


def test_long_range_precision_at_l_shape_mismatch_raises() -> None:
    from protlms.eval.contacts import long_range_precision_at_l

    with pytest.raises(ValueError):
        long_range_precision_at_l(np.zeros((4, 4)), np.zeros((4, 4), dtype=bool), np.arange(3))
```

2. Run `python -m pytest tests/test_eval_contacts.py::test_long_range_precision_at_l_default_top_is_residue_count_not_pair_count -v` and CONFIRM IT FAILS against the current buggy code (it should compute 6/15≈0.4, not 0.5). Capture the RED output.

3. Apply the fix in `src/protlms/eval/contacts.py` — replace the single `k = ...` line with:
```python
    k = n if top is None else int(top)
    k = min(k, i.size)
```
(so `top=None` → k = N residues = L, then clipped to the number of eligible pairs). Do NOT change anything else about the function (the `nan`-on-empty guard, the ValueError, the argsort, the `>=` filter all stay).

4. Run the full test file: `python -m pytest tests/test_eval_contacts.py -v` — all green (the pre-existing default-case test at sep=3 still passes because there n=6 == eligible count=6).

5. `ruff check src/ tests/ && ruff format src/ tests/ && ty check src/` — clean.

6. Commit: `fix(eval): precision@L default top is L=N residues, not eligible-pair count`.

Work from: `/home/briney/git/protlms`

## Report
APPEND your fix report to `/home/briney/git/protlms/.superpowers/sdd/task-6-report.md` with: the RED output (buggy value), the GREEN output after the fix, the covering tests you ran, the command, and the files changed.

Then reply with ONLY (under 12 lines): Status (DONE | BLOCKED); commit (short SHA + subject); one-line test summary (name the covering tests + result); the report file path.

pred nonzero entries: (0,1)=0.9, (0,2)=0.8, (0,3)=0.7, (0,4)=0.6, (0,5)=0.5, (1,2)=0.4. All other 9 pairs = 0.0.

true entries: (0,1), (0,3), (0,5), (1,3), (2,4), (3,5) = True (6 total).

Default: `top=None` → `k = n = 6`, clipped to `min(6, 15) = 6`.

Rank by descending pred (stable for ties, and among the zero-score pairs order preserves `np.triu_indices` order i.e. row-major (i,j) with i<j):

1. (0,1)=0.9 → true
2. (0,2)=0.8 → false
3. (0,3)=0.7 → true
4. (0,4)=0.6 → false
5. (0,5)=0.5 → true
6. (1,2)=0.4 → false

Top-6 = {(0,1)T, (0,2)F, (0,3)T, (0,4)F, (0,5)T, (1,2)F} → 3 true / 6 = **0.5**. Matches assertion `== 0.5`. ✓

Now check what scoring all 15 eligible pairs would give (i.e., the old buggy `k = i.size = 15` behavior): all 6 true pairs are somewhere among the 15 (regardless of rank, since k=15 includes everything), so precision = 6/15 ≈ 0.4. This confirms the test **would fail under the old code** (0.4 ≠ 0.5), and the report's own RED-phase log (`assert 0.4 == 0.5`) confirms this empirically. The test genuinely distinguishes the two behaviors. ✓

**Step 3: Shape-mismatch test.**
```python
long_range_precision_at_l(np.zeros((4, 4)), np.zeros((4, 4), dtype=bool), np.arange(3))
```
Here `pred.shape=(4,4)` so `n=4`; `true.shape=(4,4)` matches `(n,n)`; but `resnums.shape=(3,)` ≠ `(n,)=(4,)` → triggers the `ValueError` branch as coded. Wrapped in `pytest.raises(ValueError)`. Correctly exercises the intended path. ✓

**Step 4: Pre-existing `test_long_range_precision_at_l_known_case` default-top assertion.**

n=6, resnums=0..5, sep=3 → eligible pairs (|i-j|>=3): (0,3),(0,4),(0,5),(1,4),(1,5),(2,5) → `i.size = 6`. Default: `k = n = 6`, clipped to `min(6,6)=6` → unchanged from before the fix. True contacts among these 6: (0,3) and (1,5) → 2/6. Matches `pytest.approx(2/6)`. Confirmed unaffected by the fix, as claimed. ✓

**Step 5: Spec compliance overall.**

- `>=` separation: `np.abs(resnums[i]-resnums[j]) >= sep` ✓
- Descending stable rank: `np.argsort(-pred[i,j], kind="stable")` ✓
- `nan` on zero eligible pairs: `if i.size == 0: return float("nan")` ✓ (checked before computing `k`, so no div-by-zero risk even in edge cases)
- `ValueError` on shape mismatch: present, checks all three arrays against `(n,n)`/`(n,)` ✓
- Default `top=None` → `top = N` (residue count), clipped to eligible-pair count: fixed correctly ✓
- Explicit `top` → `int(top)`, clipped to eligible-pair count: ✓
- Exported from `eval/__init__.py`: added to both import and `__all__` ✓

One thing worth noting for completeness: if a caller passes `top=0` or a negative `top`, `k = int(top)` then `min(k, i.size)` could yield `k<=0`, causing a `ZeroDivisionError` or nonsensical negative-count division at the final `/ float(k)` line. This is out of scope per the brief (which only specifies `top: int | None`, no explicit bounds-validation requirement), and isn't part of the bug being fixed, so it's a pre-existing minor gap rather than a regression.

### Spec Compliance
- ✅ Spec compliant

### Strengths
- The `k` fix is minimal and precise: only the `k = ...` line was touched, with a clean two-line rewrite (`k = n if top is None else int(top)`, then `k = min(k, i.size)`) that correctly separates "compute intended L" from "clip to eligible pairs," exactly matching the brief's binding semantics.
- The new regression test (`test_long_range_precision_at_l_default_top_is_residue_count_not_pair_count`) is well constructed: sep=1 forces all 15 pairs eligible while N=6, cleanly separating the two candidate behaviors (0.5 vs 0.4) that were previously indistinguishable in the n==eligible-count coincidence of the original test.
- RED-phase evidence in the report (`assert 0.4 == 0.5`) independently corroborates that the test fails on the pre-fix code, not just in theory.
- The shape-mismatch test and its `ValueError` path are correctly wired and add real coverage that was previously untested.
- Pre-existing tests were re-verified by hand to still hold under the new code, and the reasoning (n == eligible count in that fixture) is correct.
- Export change in `__init__.py` is complete and consistent (import + `__all__`).

### Issues

#### Critical (Must Fix)
None.

#### Important (Should Fix)
None.

#### Minor (Nice to Have)
- `src/protlms/eval/contacts.py:94-97` — no validation that `top` is positive; `top=0` or a negative `top` propagates to `k = min(k, i.size)` and then `float(true[...].sum()) / float(k)`, which either divides by zero or by a negative number instead of raising a clear error. Not part of this bug's scope and not required by the brief, but worth a follow-up guard (e.g., raise `ValueError` for `top <= 0`) if the function is ever exposed to untrusted/CLI input.

### Assessment
**Task quality:** Approved
**Reasoning:** The `k` computation now correctly implements `top=None ⇒ L=N` clipped to the eligible-pair count, matching the brief's binding semantics exactly; the new regression and shape-mismatch tests were hand-traced and confirmed to genuinely exercise the fixed behavior (including independent confirmation that the old code would fail them), and the two pre-existing tests still pass under the corrected logic for the reasons stated.</result>

## Context

- ⚠️ Cannot verify from diff: real-world numerical agreement with the original Zhang/Ovchinnikov reference implementation (e.g. ESM-IF/`esm.utils.contacts`) beyond what the brief's pseudocode specifies — no reference fixture or golden-value test is present, so correctness rests on the pseudocode transcription itself, not an independent oracle.

Verified line-by-line against `containers/esm/entrypoint.py:113-133`:
1. Centering over all 4 axes (`for axis in range(4): j = j - j.mean(axis=axis, keepdims=True)`) — matches spec order/axes exactly (non-in-place rebind, safer than the spec's literal `-=` since it avoids mutating an aliased input array — see Strengths).
2. 4-D symmetrize `(j + j.transpose(2, 3, 0, 1)) / 2.0` — exact transpose permutation match.
3. Frobenius norm over AA axes `np.sqrt((j**2).sum(axis=(1, 3)))` → `(L, L)` — exact axis match.
4. Diagonal zeroed via `np.fill_diagonal(contacts, 0.0)` before APC.
5. APC: `a1 = contacts.sum(axis=0, keepdims=True)`, `a2 = contacts.sum(axis=1, keepdims=True)`, `contacts - (a1 * a2) / contacts.sum()` — correct broadcast orientation, computed post-diagonal-zeroing per spec order; diagonal re-zeroed after.
6. Final `(L,L)` symmetrize `(contacts + contacts.T) / 2.0`, cast to `float32` via `.astype(np.float32)`.

Numerically verified with a standalone reproduction (`np.random.default_rng` seeded inputs) that the implementation: produces the correct `(L,L)` shape, is symmetric, has a zero diagonal, and returns `float32`.

`_AA_ORDER = "ACDEFGHIKLMNPQRSTVWY"` (entrypoint.py:105) — confirmed 20 unique letters, no duplicates, exact match to spec string. `aa_token_ids` (entrypoint.py:108-110) maps each letter via `tokenizer.convert_tokens_to_ids`, returns a 20-element list — matches spec and test (`test_aa_token_ids_maps_twenty_amino_acids`, tests/test_esm_entrypoint.py:89-95) using a fake tokenizer.

**Permutation-invariance test reasoning (verified empirically, not just asserted):** I constructed several deliberately-broken variants of the pipeline to probe what the test (`tests/test_esm_entrypoint.py:76-86`) actually detects:
- Skipping the AA-axis centering, skipping the 4-D symmetrize step, using wrong axes in APC, and using `abs` instead of sum-of-squares all **still pass** the permutation-invariance test — because permuting axes 1 and 3 identically and then summing over those same axes is invariant regardless of bugs confined to the L-axes (0, 2) or the (L,L)-only APC step.
- A bug that indexes a *specific* AA channel instead of reducing over all of them (e.g., `j[:, 0, :, 0]` instead of `(j**2).sum(axis=(1,3))`) **does fail** the test (max diff ≈ 0.53).

Conclusion: the test is genuine, not vacuous, but its detection power is narrow — it only catches bugs in how the AA axes are reduced/aggregated (e.g., wrong axis choice for the Frobenius sum, or an off-by-one/asymmetric handling of axes 1 vs 3). It provides no independent signal on the correctness of the L-axis centering, the 4-D transpose symmetrization, or the APC formula — those are validated only by direct code inspection against the spec text, and by the shape/symmetry/zero-diagonal test (`tests/test_esm_entrypoint.py:63-73`). This matches the brief's own framing exactly, so the test does what it claims to do — no overclaiming.

### Strengths
- The centering step uses `j = j - j.mean(...)` (rebind) rather than the spec's literal `j -= j.mean(...)` (in-place). Since `np.asarray(jac, dtype=np.float64)` can return a view/alias of the caller's array when `jac` is already `float64` (e.g., the test's `rng.standard_normal` output), an in-place `-=` would silently mutate the caller's input array. The implementer's non-in-place version avoids this aliasing hazard while being numerically identical — a good defensive deviation from the literal pseudocode.
- `fill_diagonal` and all mutations operate only on the locally-produced `contacts` array, never on `jac` — no aliasing bugs on the input.
- Correctly follows the existing file convention of lazy-importing heavy/optional dependencies inside functions (`import numpy as np` inside `jacobian_to_contacts`, entrypoint.py:120), consistent with the module's stated "dependency-light at import time" design (entrypoint.py:10-12).
- Test suite grew from 11 to 14 tests, all passing; ruff check/format clean (independently reverified).
- `_AA_ORDER` verified programmatically: exactly 20 unique letters, correct standard alphabet.

### Issues

#### Critical (Must Fix)
None.

#### Important (Should Fix)
None.

#### Minor (Nice to Have)
- `containers/esm/entrypoint.py:108,113` — `aa_token_ids` and `jacobian_to_contacts` have no type hints on `jac`/return (`# noqa: ANN001, ANN201`), despite the file already having `from __future__ import annotations` (entrypoint.py:15), which makes annotations lazy strings — `jac: np.ndarray` / `-> np.ndarray` could have been added with zero runtime cost (no need to import numpy at module level) while satisfying the project's "type hints on all function signatures" convention. This was copied verbatim from the brief's own suggested code, so it's a brief-level gap rather than an implementer error, but it's an easy improvement.
- `tests/test_esm_entrypoint.py:64,77` — `import numpy as np` is repeated inside each of the two new test functions rather than hoisted to the module-level import block (entrypoint.py test file has no top-level numpy import). Purely cosmetic; matches the brief's literal test code.
- `containers/esm/entrypoint.py:113` — no reference/golden-value test against a known-correct output (e.g., a small hand-computed or externally-sourced contact map) exists to validate the pipeline against ground truth beyond re-deriving the spec's own pseudocode; current tests validate internal consistency (shape/symmetry/zero-diag/permutation-invariance) but not absolute correctness against the original Zhang/Ovchinnikov reference output. Not required by the brief, so not weighted as Important, but worth noting as a coverage gap for future hardening.

### Assessment
**Task quality:** Approved
**Reasoning:** The implementation is a faithful, line-for-line correct port of the specified 6-step pipeline (verified by direct inspection and independent numerical reproduction), the AA alphabet mapping is correct with no duplicates, and the permutation-invariance test — while narrow in scope, as reasoned and empirically confirmed above — is genuinely meaningful rather than vacuous. All identified issues are minor/cosmetic and inherited from the brief's own suggested code rather than implementer error.</result>

input.prompt:
You are implementing Task 13: Integration — ESM `contacts` + end-to-end CASP14 eval (Docker, slow). This is the FINAL task and the first real execution of the container's `categorical_jacobian`.

## Task Description
Read your task brief first — it is your requirements, with the exact test code to use verbatim:
`/home/briney/git/protlms/.superpowers/sdd/task-13-brief.md`

## Environment (important)
- Docker daemon is AVAILABLE. The image `ghcr.io/briney/protlms-esm:t6_8M` is NOT yet built.
- Use `python -m pytest ...` (bare `pytest` is the wrong linuxbrew interpreter). `python`/`pip` on PATH are the correct conda env.
- These tests are `@pytest.mark.slow` and gated by `PROTLMS_RUN_DOCKER_TESTS=1` (already set in the file's `pytestmark`). They only run when that env var is set.

## Context
Append integration tests to `tests/test_integration_esm.py` (which already builds the esm image via its `esm_image` fixture and drives the real client). Add (per the brief, verbatim):
- `test_manifest_now_declares_contacts` (manifest capabilities include "contacts").
- `test_contacts_end_to_end_shapes` (model.contacts on TINY_FASTA → (n,n) float32 finite symmetric maps for insulin_b/gb1/melittin).
- `test_evaluate_contacts_casp14_target` (copy tests/data/casp14/T1024.pdb into a temp dir, run `evaluate_contacts(model, pdb_dir, max_length=400)`, assert one TargetResult with 0<=P@L<=1 and mean not nan).

## Running it (STAGE to avoid the 600s bash timeout)
The docker build (~5-6 min) plus the T1024 391-residue categorical Jacobian on CPU (~several min) can exceed a single command's time budget. So:
1. FIRST build the image in its own command (give it up to 600000ms):
   `docker build --build-arg ESM_HF_ID=facebook/esm2_t6_8M_UR50D --build-arg ESM_MODEL_NAME=esm2_t6_8M --build-arg ESM_MODEL_FAMILY=esm2 -t ghcr.io/briney/protlms-esm:t6_8M containers/esm`
2. Then run the fast integration tests first (image now present, fixture won't rebuild):
   `PROTLMS_RUN_DOCKER_TESTS=1 python -m pytest tests/test_integration_esm.py -m slow -k "manifest or contacts_end_to_end or (embed or likelihood or score)" -v` (the contacts-shapes test on TINY_FASTA is quick).
3. Then run the slow CASP14 eval test on its own (may take several minutes — that's expected):
   `PROTLMS_RUN_DOCKER_TESTS=1 python -m pytest tests/test_integration_esm.py -m slow -k evaluate_contacts_casp14 -v` (use up to 600000ms).
   Report the ACTUAL precision@L value observed for T1024 (a real scientific result; the tiny 8M model may give a modest but >0 value — the test only asserts 0<=P@L<=1 and not nan).

If the T1024 eval genuinely can't finish within the time budget even alone, report DONE_WITH_CONCERNS: keep the (passing) manifest + contacts-shapes tests as proof `categorical_jacobian` runs correctly end-to-end, and describe the eval-test timeout so the controller can decide (e.g. use a shorter target). Do NOT delete or weaken the eval test to make it pass.

## Then
- `ruff check tests/ && ruff format tests/` — clean.
- Commit with the message from the brief's final step.
- Self-review.

Work from: `/home/briney/git/protlms`

## Report
Write your full report (tests added, the docker build result, EACH integration test's result, the ACTUAL T1024 precision@L observed, any timeout notes, files changed) to:
`/home/briney/git/protlms/.superpowers/sdd/task-13-report.md`

Then reply with ONLY (under 15 lines): Status (DONE | DONE_WITH_CONCERNS | BLOCKED); commit (short SHA + subject); one-line test summary incl. the observed T1024 P@L; concerns if any; the report file path.

input.prompt:
You are a Senior Code Reviewer. Review a completed feature branch against its spec/plan and code-quality standards before merge. This is the final whole-branch review.

## What Was Implemented
A new `contacts` capability for the `protlms` toolkit: categorical-Jacobian residue-contact prediction (Zhang/Ovchinnikov, PNAS 2024), plus an in-package `protlms.eval` harness that scores long-range precision@L on CASP14, for the ESM family (ESM-1b + all ESM-2 sizes). The container computes the full (L,20,L,20) Jacobian and returns a tiny (L,L) contact map; the ML-free client validates/stages/drives/parses; the eval module (numpy+biopython) builds ground-truth contacts from PDBs and computes the metric. The ESM2 image was generalized into a shared `containers/esm/` image (ESM-1b + ESM-2). Contract bumped 0.3 → 0.4.

## Requirements / Plan
- Spec: `docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-01-contacts-categorical-jacobian-design.md`
- Plan: `docs/superpowers/plans/2026-07-01-contacts-categorical-jacobian-esm.md`
Read both. Note the plan's Global Constraints (contract mirror rule; client no-ML-deps except numpy+biopython; contract 0.4; `contacts`/`contact_map`/`categorical-jacobian` names; metric = Cβ<8Å, long-range |i−j|≥24, precision@L with L=N residues). ESM-C migration is explicitly a SEPARATE future plan (not in this branch) — its absence is not a gap.

## Git Range to Review
**Base (merge-base main):** 67dbd5a
**Head:** dd0d6ea
**Diff file (read this — commit list + stat + full diff with context):** `/home/briney/git/protlms/.superpowers/sdd/review-67dbd5a..dd0d6ea.diff`
The diff is ~438KB ONLY because it adds a real ~244KB CASP14 structure `tests/data/casp14/T1024.pdb` (thousands of ATOM lines) — skim that; focus on the code. Read the diff file once rather than re-running git across the range. For any file you must inspect beyond the diff context, use `git show`/Read (read-only). Do NOT run the Docker-gated (slow) tests — they take ~12 min; the controller confirmed they pass (image built; 9/9 fast integration + CASP14 eval T1024 precision@L=0.0614 in 302s).

## Read-Only Review
Read-only on this checkout. Do not mutate the working tree, index, HEAD, or branch state.

## Verification already done (don't redo)
Controller confirmed on the branch HEAD: full fast suite 226 passed / 32 slow-deselected; `ruff check src/ tests/ containers/esm/entrypoint.py` clean; `ruff format --check` clean; `ty check src/` clean. The per-task reviews all passed (spec ✅ + quality Approved), including a hand-verified `categorical_jacobian` tensor construction and a hand-verified precision@L bugfix.

## Focus areas for a whole-branch lens (things per-task reviews can't see)
- Cross-cutting consistency: does `Model.contacts` / `ContactsResult` / `load_contact_maps` / the `contact_map` artifact / the container's `write_contacts_outputs` output all agree on the same shape/dtype/keys end-to-end? Does the client correctly parse what the container writes?
- Contract mirror integrity: do `contract.py` and `docs/CONTRACT.md` agree, and does the worked example match `tests/data/result.contacts.example.json`?
- The categorical-Jacobian science pipeline correctness across `categorical_jacobian` → `jacobian_to_contacts` → `long_range_precision_at_l` (axes/order/definitions), and whether the eval's resolved-residue + residue-number-separation gap policy is coherent.
- Any real code path still pointing at the removed `containers/esm2`.
- Backward/contract compatibility of the 0.4 bump.

## Triage this roll-up of Minor findings from per-task reviews — decide which (if any) must be fixed before merge vs. deferred:
1. Doc drift: top-level `CLAUDE.md`, `README.md`, and `docs/CONTRACT.md` ("Adding a model" §, ~line 327) still reference `containers/esm2/`/`ESM2_CHECKPOINT` after the rename to `containers/esm`.
2. Stale plan-doc snippet: the plan's Task 6 Step 3 code still shows the pre-fix `k = i.size if top is None else ...` line; shipped code is fixed (commit 99072cb) — plan doc not updated.
3. `long_range_precision_at_l` has no guard for `top<=0` (CLI `--top` exposes it) → would divide by zero.
4. `Model.contacts` exposes `--no-pull` (via CLI) while sibling `score` does not — minor CLI inconsistency.
5. `docs/CONTRACT.md` §4 "Inputs" bullet doesn't mention `contacts` reuses `/in/seqs.fasta`.
6. Container helpers `aa_token_ids`/`jacobian_to_contacts`/`categorical_jacobian` lack type hints (use `# noqa: ANN...`, inert since ANN not in ruff select) — matches file's existing convention.
7. No golden-value/reference test for `jacobian_to_contacts` (validated by internal-consistency + the end-to-end CASP14 smoke only).
8. esm1b registry image tag `esm1b_650M` breaks the `t<layers>_<size>` tag convention.

## Calibration
Categorize by ACTUAL severity; acknowledge strengths first. Flag any genuine plan-vs-implementation deviation. If an issue is with the plan itself rather than the code, say so.

## Output Format
### Strengths
### Issues
#### Critical (Must Fix)
#### Important (Should Fix)
#### Minor (Nice to Have)
(each: file:line, what's wrong, why it matters, how to fix)
### Recommendations
### Assessment
**Ready to merge?** [Yes | No | With fixes]
**Reasoning:** [1-2 sentences]

DON'T say "looks good" without checking; DON'T mark nitpicks Critical; be specific (file:line); give a clear verdict.

I now have everything needed. Let me compile the review.

## Review Complete

### Strengths

- **End-to-end shape/dtype/key consistency is airtight.** The `contact_map` artifact contract is honored identically at every layer: container `write_contacts_outputs` writes `contacts/<id>.npy` with `record_ids=[id]`, `shape=[L,L]`, `dtype="float32"` (entrypoint.py:443-462); `io.load_contact_maps` keys by `record_ids[0]` and loads that path (io.py); `ContactsResult.maps()` delegates to it (models.py:126-128); and the worked example `tests/data/result.contacts.example.json` matches the `docs/CONTRACT.md` §Outputs worked example byte-for-byte. `stage_inputs` uses `seqs.fasta`, exactly matching the container's `contacts --input /in/seqs.fasta`.

- **The categorical-Jacobian science pipeline is correct.** I hand-verified the axes: `categorical_jacobian` produces `jac (n_src, a_src, j_tgt, b_tgt)` and `jac -= baseline` correctly broadcasts the `(L,20)` WT baseline over the last two axes `(j_tgt, b_tgt)` (entrypoint.py:500). `jacobian_to_contacts` implements the spec's exact 6-step order — 4-axis centering, `transpose(2,3,0,1)` symmetrization, Frobenius over the two AA axes `(1,3)`, zero-diag, APC, final symmetrization (entrypoint.py:113-133). The tokenizer-driven `aa_token_ids` + special-token-mask residue detection correctly generalizes the reference's hardcoded ESM `4:24` block.

- **Gap policy is coherent and correct.** I confirmed CASP14 T1024 has real residue-number gaps (194→203, 250→257; 391 resolved residues spanning numbers 2–406). The design is sound: `pred` (container, array-index 0..390) and `true` (`true_contact_map` from `cb_coords`, same array order) share one index space, while `resnums` maps array-index→PDB-number *only* for the separation filter `|resnum_i − resnum_j| ≥ sep`. This matches the reference's "restrict to PDB residues" and the spec exactly.

- **Contract 0.4 bump is backward-compatible.** Same major version, `extra="ignore"` on all models, `check_contract_compatibility` only enforces major-version match — a 0.3 client and 0.4 image stay mutually readable as intended. `contract.py` and `docs/CONTRACT.md` are in sync (capability, artifact kind, subcommand block, outputs row, worked example).

- **No live code path references the removed `containers/esm2`.** The directory is gone; `models.yaml` points all ESM entries at `containers/esm` / `protlms-esm`. The `protlms-esm2` strings remaining in `tests/test_runner.py`, `test_registry.py`, `test_registry_publish.py`, `test_models.py` are synthetic fixture literals, not registry lookups — harmless. The publish workflow is data-driven from `models.yaml` (no hardcoded matrix).

### Issues

#### Critical (Must Fix)
None.

#### Important (Should Fix)

- **`long_range_precision_at_l` divides by zero / returns garbage for `top<=0` — reachable via the CLI (`src/protlms/eval/contacts.py:168-171`).** This is triage item #3, and I confirmed it is real by executing the metric logic in isolation:
  - `top=0` → `ZeroDivisionError: division by zero`. This is **not** a `ProtlmsError`, so the CLI's `except ProtlmsError` in `eval_contacts` (cli.py:329) will not catch it — the user gets a raw traceback instead of a clean exit-1.
  - `top=-2` → silently returns `-0.5` (`k = min(-2, size) = -2`; `order[:-2]` slices; divides by `-2`), a meaningless negative "precision."

  `protlms eval contacts ... --top 0` (or a negative) is a plausible user typo. Fix: guard at the top of the function, e.g. `if top is not None and top <= 0: raise ValueError(f"top must be positive, got {top}")` (or `InvalidRequestError` so the CLI catches it cleanly). Add a parametrized test for `top=0` and `top=-1`. This is the one behavioral defect in genuinely new code with a user-facing path; everything else is cosmetic.

#### Minor (Nice to Have)

- **Doc drift: stale `containers/esm2/` / `ESM2_CHECKPOINT` references (triage #1).** Confirmed at: `docs/CONTRACT.md:327` ("See `containers/esm2/` for a reference implementation"); `CLAUDE.md:93-94`; `README.md:24,28-30,36,53`. None affect runtime, but `docs/CONTRACT.md:327` is the most visible (it's the normative contract doc's "Adding a model" section pointing at a directory that no longer exists). Recommend fixing at least the CONTRACT.md and README build-command lines. Note: `CLAUDE.md` is checked-in project instructions — updating it keeps future agents accurate.

- **`docs/CONTRACT.md` §Inputs (lines 64-68) omits `contacts` (triage #5).** The subcommand block (line 59) and Outputs table (line 145) both cover `contacts`, but the Inputs bullet list never states that `contacts` reads `/in/seqs.fasta`. One-line addition for completeness.

- **`--no-pull` inconsistency is pre-existing, not introduced here (triage #4).** `score` (cli.py:185) calls `load(model)` with no pull control, while every *other* command — including the new `contacts` (line 211) and `eval contacts` (line 302) — passes `allow_pull=False if no_pull else None`. The new code is the consistent side; `score` is the outlier. Worth a one-line fix to `score` for uniformity, but it's not a regression from this branch.

- **`esm1b` image tag `esm1b_650M` breaks the `t<layers>_<size>` convention (triage #8, `models.yaml:21`).** All ESM-2 tags are `t<N>_<size>` (e.g. `t33_650M`); ESM-1b uses `esm1b_650M`. Cosmetic — the tag is arbitrary and self-consistent with the build-arg `ESM_MODEL_NAME=esm1b_t33_650M`, but a purist would use `t33_650M` scoped by the family in the repo name. Low value; leave or normalize at will.

- **No golden-value reference test for `jacobian_to_contacts` (triage #7).** Currently validated only by internal-consistency properties (shape/symmetry/zero-diag/AA-permutation-invariance) plus the CASP14 smoke test. A tiny hand-computed 2×2 or 3×3 fixture would lock the exact numerics against future refactors. Nice-to-have, not blocking.

- **Type hints omitted on container helpers (triage #6)** via `# noqa: ANN...`. This matches the file's existing convention (all container helpers do this because `ANN` isn't in the ruff select set), so it's consistent by design — no action needed.

- **Stale plan-doc snippet (triage #2):** the plan's Task 6 Step 3 still shows the pre-fix `k = i.size if top is None else ...`; shipped code (contacts.py:168-169) is the corrected `k = n if top is None else int(top); k = min(k, i.size)`. This is a plan-doc-vs-code drift, not a code issue — the code is right.

### Recommendations

1. Fix the `top<=0` guard in `long_range_precision_at_l` before merge (raise a caught, structured error) and add a regression test — this is the only functional defect.
2. Batch the doc-drift fixes (CONTRACT.md:327, README build lines, CLAUDE.md:93-94, CONTRACT.md Inputs bullet) into one small commit; they're all trivial and improve the contract doc's accuracy.
3. Optionally normalize the `score` command's `load(...)` call to accept `--no-pull` for CLI uniformity.

### Assessment

**Ready to merge?** With fixes

**Reasoning:** The architecture, cross-layer contract consistency, and the load-bearing categorical-Jacobian science (axes, baseline broadcast, post-processing order, gap policy) are all correct and I verified them by hand and by executing the metric/tensor logic. The only functional defect is the unguarded `top<=0` divide-by-zero in `long_range_precision_at_l`, which is reachable via `protlms eval contacts --top` and escapes the CLI's error handling — fix that (Important) plus the cheap doc-drift items, and this is a clean merge. All other triage findings are cosmetic or pre-existing.</result>

The final review verdict: **Ready to merge — With fixes.** The reviewer hand-verified the science (axes, baseline broadcast, pipeline order, gap policy) — all correct, no Critical. There's **one Important defect** worth fixing before merge:

- **`long_range_precision_at_l` doesn't guard `top<=0`** — `--top 0` raises a bare `ZeroDivisionError` (not a `ProtlmsError`, so the CLI's handler misses it → raw traceback), and `--top -2` silently returns a nonsensical negative "precision." Reachable via `protlms eval contacts --top`.

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /home/briney/.claude/plugins/cache/claude-plugins-official/superpowers/6.1.0/skills/finishing-a-development-branch

# Finishing a Development Branch

## Overview

Guide completion of development work by presenting clear options and handling chosen workflow.

**Core principle:** Verify tests → Detect environment → Present options → Execute choice → Clean up.

**Announce at start:** "I'm using the finishing-a-development-branch skill to complete this work."

## The Process

### Step 1: Verify Tests

**Before presenting options, verify tests pass:**

```bash
# Run project's test suite
npm test / cargo test / pytest / go test ./...
```

**If tests fail:**
```
Tests failing (<N> failures). Must fix before completing:

[Show failures]

Cannot proceed with merge/PR until tests pass.
```

Stop. Don't proceed to Step 2.

**If tests pass:** Continue to Step 2.

### Step 2: Detect Environment

**Determine workspace state before presenting options:**

```bash
GIT_DIR=$(cd "$(git rev-parse --git-dir)" 2>/dev/null && pwd -P)
GIT_COMMON=$(cd "$(git rev-parse --git-common-dir)" 2>/dev/null && pwd -P)
```

This determines which menu to show and how cleanup works:

| State | Menu | Cleanup |
|-------|------|---------|
| `GIT_DIR == GIT_COMMON` (normal repo) | Standard 4 options | No worktree to clean up |
| `GIT_DIR != GIT_COMMON`, named branch | Standard 4 options | Provenance-based (see Step 6) |
| `GIT_DIR != GIT_COMMON`, detached HEAD | Reduced 3 options (no merge) | No cleanup (externally managed) |

### Step 3: Determine Base Branch

```bash
# Try common base branches
git merge-base HEAD main 2>/dev/null || git merge-base HEAD master 2>/dev/null
```

Or ask: "This branch split from main - is that correct?"

### Step 4: Present Options

**Normal repo and named-branch worktree — present exactly these 4 options:**

```
Implementation complete. What would you like to do?

1. Merge back to <base-branch> locally
2. Push and create a Pull Request
3. Keep the branch as-is (I'll handle it later)
4. Discard this work

Which option?
```

**Detached HEAD — present exactly these 3 options:**

```
Implementation complete. You're on a detached HEAD (externally managed workspace).

1. Push as new branch and create a Pull Request
2. Keep as-is (I'll handle it later)
3. Discard this work

Which option?
```

**Don't add explanation** - keep options concise.

### Step 5: Execute Choice

#### Option 1: Merge Locally

```bash
# Get main repo root for CWD safety
MAIN_ROOT=$(git -C "$(git rev-parse --git-common-dir)/.." rev-parse --show-toplevel)
cd "$MAIN_ROOT"

# Merge first — verify success before removing anything
git checkout <base-branch>
git pull
git merge <feature-branch>

# Verify tests on merged result
<test command>

# Only after merge succeeds: cleanup worktree (Step 6), then delete branch
```

Then: Cleanup worktree (Step 6), then delete branch:

```bash
git branch -d <feature-branch>
```

#### Option 2: Push and Create PR

```bash
# Push branch
git push -u origin <feature-branch>
```

**Do NOT clean up worktree** — user needs it alive to iterate on PR feedback.

#### Option 3: Keep As-Is

Report: "Keeping branch <name>. Worktree preserved at <path>."

**Don't cleanup worktree.**

#### Option 4: Discard

**Confirm first:**
```
This will permanently delete:
- Branch <name>
- All commits: <commit-list>
- Worktree at <path>

Type 'discard' to confirm.
```

Wait for exact confirmation.

If confirmed:
```bash
MAIN_ROOT=$(git -C "$(git rev-parse --git-common-dir)/.." rev-parse --show-toplevel)
cd "$MAIN_ROOT"
```

Then: Cleanup worktree (Step 6), then force-delete branch:
```bash
git branch -D <feature-branch>
```

### Step 6: Cleanup Workspace

**Only runs for Options 1 and 4.** Options 2 and 3 always preserve the worktree.

```bash
GIT_DIR=$(cd "$(git rev-parse --git-dir)" 2>/dev/null && pwd -P)
GIT_COMMON=$(cd "$(git rev-parse --git-common-dir)" 2>/dev/null && pwd -P)
WORKTREE_PATH=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
```

**If `GIT_DIR == GIT_COMMON`:** Normal repo, no worktree to clean up. Done.

**If worktree path is under `.worktrees/` or `worktrees/`:** Superpowers created this worktree — we own cleanup.

```bash
MAIN_ROOT=$(git -C "$(git rev-parse --git-common-dir)/.." rev-parse --show-toplevel)
cd "$MAIN_ROOT"
git worktree remove "$WORKTREE_PATH"
git worktree prune  # Self-healing: clean up any stale registrations
```

**Otherwise:** The host environment (harness) owns this workspace. Do NOT remove it. If your platform provides a workspace-exit tool, use it. Otherwise, leave the workspace in place.

## Quick Reference

| Option | Merge | Push | Keep Worktree | Cleanup Branch |
|--------|-------|------|---------------|----------------|
| 1. Merge locally | yes | - | - | yes |
| 2. Create PR | - | yes | yes | - |
| 3. Keep as-is | - | - | yes | - |
| 4. Discard | - | - | - | yes (force) |

## Common Mistakes

**Skipping test verification**
- **Problem:** Merge broken code, create failing PR
- **Fix:** Always verify tests before offering options

**Open-ended questions**
- **Problem:** "What should I do next?" is ambiguous
- **Fix:** Present exactly 4 structured options (or 3 for detached HEAD)

**Cleaning up worktree for Option 2**
- **Problem:** Remove worktree user needs for PR iteration
- **Fix:** Only cleanup for Options 1 and 4

**Deleting branch before removing worktree**
- **Problem:** `git branch -d` fails because worktree still references the branch
- **Fix:** Merge first, remove worktree, then delete branch

**Running git worktree remove from inside the worktree**
- **Problem:** Command fails silently when CWD is inside the worktree being removed
- **Fix:** Always `cd` to main repo root before `git worktree remove`

**Cleaning up harness-owned worktrees**
- **Problem:** Removing a worktree the harness created causes phantom state
- **Fix:** Only clean up worktrees under `.worktrees/` or `worktrees/`

**No confirmation for discard**
- **Problem:** Accidentally delete work
- **Fix:** Require typed "discard" confirmation

## Red Flags

**Never:**
- Proceed with failing tests
- Merge without verifying tests on result
- Delete work without confirmation
- Force-push without explicit request
- Remove a worktree before confirming merge success
- Clean up worktrees you didn't create (provenance check)
- Run `git worktree remove` from inside the worktree

**Always:**
- Verify tests before offering options
- Detect environment before presenting menu
- Present exactly 4 options (or 3 for detached HEAD)
- Get typed confirmation for Option 4
- Clean up worktree for Options 1 & 4 only
- `cd` to main repo root before worktree removal
- Run `git worktree prune` after removal
I'm using the finishing-a-development-branch skill to complete this work. First, verify tests and detect the environment.

## Summary

Adds a **`contacts`** capability to the protlms container contract and an in-package **`protlms.eval`** harness that scores emergent structural knowledge as **long-range precision@L** on CASP14, for the **ESM family** (ESM-1b + all ESM-2 sizes).

The container computes the full categorical Jacobian internally (mutate each position to all 20 AAs → logits → `(L,20,L,20)` → center → symmetrize → Frobenius → APC, per [Zhang & Ovchinnikov, PNAS 2024](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.2406285121)) and returns a tiny `(L,L)` contact-score matrix. The ML-free client validates/stages/drives/parses; the eval module (numpy + biopython) builds ground-truth contacts from PDBs and computes the metric. A full CASP14 sweep is **one container run per model**.

> Design: `docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-01-contacts-categorical-jacobian-design.md`
> Plan: `docs/superpowers/plans/2026-07-01-contacts-categorical-jacobian-esm.md`

## What's included

- **Contract 0.3 → 0.4** (minor, backward-compatible): `Capability.CONTACTS`, `ArtifactKind.CONTACT_MAP`, `contacts` subcommand, worked example — `contract.py` and `docs/CONTRACT.md` mirrored.
- **Client**: `Model.contacts(...) → ContactsResult.maps()`, `io.load_contact_maps`, `protlms contacts` CLI.
- **`protlms.eval`** (biopython added to core deps): `parse_pdb`, `true_contact_map` (Cβ<8Å, Cα for Gly), `long_range_precision_at_l` (|i−j|≥24, top-L, L=residue count), a CASP14 `evaluate_contacts` runner + CSV, and the `protlms eval contacts` CLI.
- **Shared `containers/esm/` image** (generalized from `containers/esm2/`) serving ESM-1b + all ESM-2 sizes via build args; `contacts` implemented via a tokenizer-driven categorical Jacobian.
- **Registry**: ESM-1b + all ESM-2 sizes (8M/35M/150M/650M/3B/15B) on the shared image.

## Verification

- Fast suite: **228 passed**; `ruff` + `ruff format` + `ty` clean.
- Docker integration (built `protlms-esm:t6_8M`): manifest declares `contacts`; end-to-end contact maps; **CASP14 T1024 precision@L = 0.0614** with the 8M model (~7× random baseline — appropriately modest for the smallest checkpoint; the benchmark's purpose is cross-size comparison).

## Notes

- **ESM-C** migration to `transformers` + `contacts` is a deliberate follow-on (Plan 2), reusing everything here.
- The categorical Jacobian is ~O(20·L³); registered `esm2-3b/15b` are opt-in and impractical for long targets — the benchmark defaults to the ≤650M class.

Built with subagent-driven development: each task implemented + independently reviewed (spec + quality), then a whole-branch review whose one Important finding (a `top<=0` divide-by-zero in the metric) was fixed and verified.

content.text:
Done. **[PR #10](https://github.com/briney/protlms/pull/10)** is open against `main`.

## What shipped

The full ESM-family `contacts` + CASP14 benchmark, built brainstorm → spec → plan → subagent-driven execution (13 tasks, each independently reviewed) → whole-branch review → PR:

- **Contract 0.4** (backward-compatible): `contacts` capability + `contact_map` artifact, `contract.py`/`CONTRACT.md` mirrored.
- **Client**: `Model.contacts` → `ContactsResult.maps()`, `io.load_contact_maps`, `protlms contacts` CLI.
- **`protlms.eval`** (biopython now core): PDB → true contacts (Cβ<8 Å), long-range precision@L (|i−j|≥24, top-L), CASP14 runner + CSV, `protlms eval contacts` CLI.
- **Shared `containers/esm/` image** (generalized from `esm2/`) serving ESM-1b + all ESM-2 sizes, with a tokenizer-driven categorical Jacobian ported verbatim from the authors' code.
- **Registry**: ESM-1b + ESM-2 8M/35M/150M/650M/3B/15B.

**Verification**: 228 fast tests pass; ruff/format/ty clean; Docker end-to-end confirmed (built `protlms-esm:t6_8M`, real T1024 precision@L = 0.0614 on the 8M model, ~7× random baseline).

## Two things the reviews caught (both fixed)
- A **precision@L default bug** in my own plan's reference code (`top=None` used eligible-pair count, not L=N residues) — the metric would have been wrong on any real protein.
- A **`top<=0` divide-by-zero** reachable via `--top`, now raising a CLI-caught `InvalidRequestError`.

## Notes for you
- **ESM-C** is the deliberate next step (Plan 2): reuses everything here, only adds its `transformers`-based container + registry entries. I confirmed the HF logits path is viable (`AutoModelForMaskedLM`); exact repo IDs are a build-time verify item. Say the word and I'll spec/build it.
- The **giants** (esm2-3B/15B) are registered but opt-in — the Jacobian is ~O(20·L³), so the benchmark defaults to ≤650M. To get real numbers, merge #10, then `protlms eval contacts esm2-650m --pdb-dir ~/projects/esm-c/data/casp14/ --out patl.csv --max-length 400`.
- Left the branch up for PR iteration (didn't merge, per your usual flow).
PR merged and branch deleted. Let's move on to Plan 2 (ESM-C).

# ESMplusplus_small Usage Guide

## (1) Repository ID and Model Loading

**Repo ID:** `Synthyra/ESMplusplus_small`

**Loading method:** Uses `AutoModelForMaskedLM` with `trust_remote_code=True` required.

Quote: "from transformers import AutoModelForMaskedLM model = AutoModelForMaskedLM.from_pretrained('Synthyra/ESMplusplus_small', trust_remote_code=True)"

## (2) Per-Position Logits

The model output includes a `.logits` attribute containing language modeling logits with shape `(batch_size, seq_len, vocab_size)`.

Quote: "print(output.logits.shape) # language modeling logits, (batch_size, seq_len, vocab_size), (2, 11, 64)"

For the small version, vocab_size is 64.

## (3) Tokenizer

The model has an integrated tokenizer accessible via `model.tokenizer`. The documentation indicates it handles standard amino acids as single-character tokens, though the page doesn't explicitly detail `convert_tokens_to_ids()` behavior.

Quote: "tokenizer = model.tokenizer sequences = ['MPRTEIN', 'MSEQWENCE'] tokenized = tokenizer(sequences, padding=True, return_tensors='pt')"

## (4) Complete Example

```python
from transformers import AutoModelForMaskedLM
model = AutoModelForMaskedLM.from_pretrained('Synthyra/ESMplusplus_small', trust_remote_code=True)
tokenizer = model.tokenizer

sequences = ['MPRTEIN', 'MSEQWENCE']
tokenized = tokenizer(sequences, padding=True, return_tensors='pt')
output = model(**tokenized)
print(output.logits.shape)  # (2, 11, 64)
```

## Model Correspondence

- **300M version:** `ESMplusplus_small` (this model)
- **6B version:** Referenced as `Synthyra/ESMplusplus_6B` for regularization tasks
- The small version corresponds to ESMC 300M parameters

content.text:
De-risking done — key findings:

- **Both** ESM-C HF families load via `AutoModelForMaskedLM.from_pretrained(..., trust_remote_code=True)` and expose **`output.logits`** `(B, L, vocab)` — exactly what the categorical Jacobian needs. All three sizes (300M/600M/6B) exist in each family.
- **Synthyra ESM++** (`Synthyra/ESMplusplus_small`=300M, `_large`=600M, `_6B`): public, no license gate, integrated `model.tokenizer`, weight-identical ports of ESM-C. Bakes into the image with **no HF token**.
- **Official EvolutionaryScale** (`EvolutionaryScale/esmc-300m-2024-12`, etc.): official provenance, same interface, but **gated** (Cambrian Open License acceptance + HF token) — so the `_prefetch` weight-bake in Docker/CI needs an `HF_TOKEN` build secret and prior license acceptance.

One decision materially changes the plan (build friction vs. provenance):

questions.question: Which HuggingFace source should the ESM-C container use for the (weight-identical) 300M/600M/6B checkpoints?

options.label: Synthyra ESM++ (Recommended)
options.description: Synthyra/ESMplusplus_small|large|6B. Public (no license gate), pure-transformers with trust_remote_code, integrated tokenizer, .logits confirmed. Bakes into the image with NO HF token — frictionless, reproducible offline builds. Downside: third-party repo + its custom modeling code (weights are ports of ESM-C).

options.label: Official EvolutionaryScale
options.description: EvolutionaryScale/esmc-300m-2024-12 etc. Official provenance, same AutoModelForMaskedLM+trust_remote_code+.logits interface. Downside: gated — the Docker _prefetch weight-bake needs an HF_TOKEN build secret + prior Cambrian-license acceptance (extra CI wiring, less reproducible).

You can now continue with these answers in mind.

"""Probe biohub ESM-C HF loading for the categorical-Jacobian entrypoint."""

import os

os.environ.setdefault(
    "HF_HOME", "/tmp/claude-1001/-home-briney-git-protlms/bf2b7b10-35fa-4759-98a9-8aca3a54d050/scratchpad/hf"
)

import torch  # noqa: E402
import transformers  # noqa: E402

REPO = "biohub/ESMC-300M"
print("transformers", transformers.__version__)

from transformers import AutoModel, AutoModelForMaskedLM, AutoTokenizer  # noqa: E402

# --- tokenizer ---
tok = None
for kw in ({"trust_remote_code": True}, {}):
    try:
        tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(REPO, **kw)
        print("tokenizer loaded with", kw or "(no kwargs)")
        break
    except Exception as e:  # noqa: BLE001
        print("tokenizer load failed", kw, "->", type(e).__name__, str(e)[:160])
assert tok is not None, "tokenizer failed"

# --- model: prefer MaskedLM (logits); fall back to AutoModel ---
model, how = None, None
for cls in (AutoModelForMaskedLM, AutoModel):
    try:
        model = cls.from_pretrained(REPO, trust_remote_code=True)
        how = cls.__name__
        print("model loaded via", how)
        break
    except Exception as e:  # noqa: BLE001
        print("model load failed via", cls.__name__, "->", type(e).__name__, str(e)[:200])
assert model is not None, "model failed"
model.eval()

seq = "MPRTEINSEQ"
enc = tok(seq, return_tensors="pt")
ids = enc["input_ids"][0].tolist()
print("input_ids len", len(ids), "for seq len", len(seq), "->", ids)
print("special tokens:", {"cls": tok.cls_token, "eos": tok.eos_token, "bos": tok.bos_token,
                          "pad": tok.pad_token, "mask": tok.mask_token, "unk": tok.unk_token})

with torch.no_grad():
    out = model(**enc, output_hidden_states=True)
print("has .logits:", hasattr(out, "logits") and out.logits is not None)
if getattr(out, "logits", None) is not None:
    print("logits shape:", tuple(out.logits.shape))
print("has hidden_states:", getattr(out, "hidden_states", None) is not None,
      "n_layers:", len(out.hidden_states) if getattr(out, "hidden_states", None) else None)

# special-tokens mask (used to find residue positions in categorical_jacobian)
try:
    m = tok.get_special_tokens_mask(ids, already_has_special_tokens=True)
    print("get_special_tokens_mask:", m, "-> n_residue_positions:", sum(1 for x in m if x == 0))
except Exception as e:  # noqa: BLE001
    print("get_special_tokens_mask FAILED:", type(e).__name__, str(e)[:160])

# AA-token mapping (used by aa_token_ids)
AA = "ACDEFGHIKLMNPQRSTVWY"
aa_ids = [tok.convert_tokens_to_ids(a) for a in AA]
unk = tok.unk_token_id
print("aa_ids:", aa_ids)
print("any AA maps to <unk>?", any(i == unk for i in aa_ids), "unk_id:", unk)
print("config vocab_size:", getattr(model.config, "vocab_size", "?"), "len(tok):", len(tok))
print("PROBE OK")

# ESMC Installation & Usage Instructions

## 1. Package Installation

Install the `esm` package from GitHub:
```
pip install esm@git+https://github.com/Biohub/esm.git@main
```

The document notes: "a PyPI release is coming soon" but provides no alternative PyPI package name currently available.

## 2. Model Loading

**Class:** `AutoModelForMaskedLM` (from `transformers`)

**Exact call:**
```py
model = AutoModelForMaskedLM.from_pretrained("biohub/ESMC-6B", device_map="auto").eval()
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("biohub/ESMC-6B")
```

No `trust_remote_code` flag is mentioned. Alternatives: `"biohub/ESMC-600M"` or `"biohub/ESMC-300M"`.

## 3. Getting Logits

```py
inputs = tokenizer(GFP, return_tensors="pt", padding=True)
inputs = {k: v.to(model.device) for k, v in inputs.items()}

with torch.inference_mode():
    output = model(**inputs)

print(f"logits shape: {tuple(output.logits.shape)}")
```

## 4. Tokenizer Usage

```py
from transformers import AutoTokenizer
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("biohub/ESMC-6B")
inputs = tokenizer(GFP, return_tensors="pt", padding=True)
```

## 5. License

The model is dual-licensed under **MIT** and **other** (see THIRD_PARTY_NOTICE.md). Specific terms require checking the linked notice document.

Quote all relevant code blocks verbatim.

content.text:
Verified recipe (from the biohub HF card + the EvolutionaryScale/Biohub `esm` repo README, cross-confirmed):

- **Weights:** `biohub/ESMC-300M`, `biohub/ESMC-600M`, `biohub/ESMC-6B` (Fill-Mask, **MIT**) — public, bakeable, all three sizes.
- **The nuance:** these are *not* pure-transformers. Native transformers has **no `esmc`** (I checked up through the latest 5.12.1), and the configs carry `auto_map: null`. Loading requires the **Biohub `esm` package** (`pip install esm@git+https://github.com/Biohub/esm.git@main`; PyPI "coming soon"), which registers `ESMCForMaskedLM` into transformers' auto-classes. Then it's the standard uniform API:
  ```python
  from transformers import AutoModelForMaskedLM, AutoTokenizer  # + import esm to register
  model = AutoModelForMaskedLM.from_pretrained("biohub/ESMC-300M").eval()
  tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("biohub/ESMC-300M")
  out = model(**tok(seq, return_tensors="pt"))   # out.logits (B, L, 64)
  ```

So the "switch to transformers" still stands and is worthwhile — it swaps the SDK's native inference for the uniform `transformers` `.logits`/`output_hidden_states` interface (what the categorical Jacobian needs) and unlocks the 6B — but the `esm` package stays as the *enabler*. That's inherent to the biohub choice you made (the pure-transformers alternative was Synthyra, which you ruled out); your MIT/official rationale still holds. I'll pin the `esm` git install to a commit SHA for reproducibility (like the E1 container pins its ref), and make **Task 1 a build-and-probe** (as the E1 plan did) to confirm the tokenizer's special-token mask + AA-token mapping before finalizing the entrypoint.

That's enough to plan with no guesses. Writing Plan 2.
